# Deep Dive: Native Astroturfing

> **Research boundary:** This material is for analysis, ethics, detection, and academic writing. It is not an operational guide for deceptive growth or platform manipulation.

## Core idea

Native astroturfing uses the language, formats, and norms of a community to make promotion look like peer discussion.

## Common surfaces

- Reddit.
- Discord communities.
- Hacker News-style forums.
- Facebook Groups.
- Product communities.
- Niche subcultures.

## Format patterns to study

| Native format | Why it works | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| “I found this” posts | Looks like discovery | Hides promotional origin |
| Comparison prompts | Invites debate | Converts users into amplifiers |
| Gameplay clips / demos | Feels experiential | Can be planted by marketers |
| Question posts | Produces comments | Can seed demand artificially |
| Meme formats | Feels community-native | Masks campaign intent |
| Soft criticism | Makes post look less promotional | Can be scripted to increase believability |

## Detection questions

- Does the account have a real community history?
- Does the content appear across multiple communities with similar framing?
- Are assets unusually polished for a casual discovery post?
- Is there later evidence of an agency, brief, or campaign page?
- Are comments organically diverse or guided toward the same claim?

## Ethical analysis

Native astroturfing exploits the trust that makes communities valuable. The harm is not just the promotional message; it is the conversion of peer trust into hidden advertising inventory.
